tagline: Calculate monthly payments for your RV loan
keywords: rv loan calculator, rv payment calculator, motorhome financing, rv monthly payment
calcFn: var loan = price - down; if (loan <= 0) return; var r = rate / 100 / 12; var n = term * 12; var payment = r > 0 ? loan * (r * Math.pow(1+r, n)) / (Math.pow(1+r, n) - 1) : loan / n; var totalPaid = payment * n; document.getElementById('monthly').textContent = dollar(payment); document.getElementById('totalInterest').textContent = dollar(totalPaid - loan); document.getElementById('totalCost').textContent = dollar(totalPaid + down); document.getElementById('loanAmount').textContent = dollar(loan); document.getElementById('resultTip').textContent = 'Total interest is ' + pct((totalPaid - loan) / loan * 100, 1) + ' of your loan amount.';
formulas: Monthly Payment = P × [r(1+r)^n] / [(1+r)^n – 1]
P = loan amount, r = monthly rate, n = total months
Total Interest = (Monthly Payment × Months) – Loan Amount

faq.question: What is a typical RV loan interest rate?
faq.answer: RV loan rates typically range from 5% to 12% depending on credit score, loan amount, and whether the RV is new or used. Excellent credit borrowers may qualify for rates under 5%.

faq.question: How long can you finance an RV?
faq.answer: RV loans can extend up to 20 years for loans over $50,000. Most common terms are 10-15 years. Shorter terms mean higher payments but significantly less total interest paid.

faq.question: How much should I put down on an RV?
faq.answer: Most lenders require 10-20% down. A larger down payment reduces your monthly payment and total interest, and may qualify you for a better rate.

tagline: Estimate fuel costs for your RV road trip
keywords: rv fuel cost calculator, rv gas mileage, motorhome fuel cost, rv trip fuel
calcFn: var gal = distance / mpg; var cost = gal * fuelPrice; var cpm = cost / distance; document.getElementById('gallons').textContent = fmt(gal, 1) + ' gal'; document.getElementById('fuelCost').textContent = dollar(cost); document.getElementById('costPerMile').textContent = dollar(cpm) + '/mi';
formulas: Gallons = Distance ÷ MPG
Fuel Cost = Gallons × Price per Gallon
Cost per Mile = Total Cost ÷ Distance

faq.question: What MPG does a typical RV get?
faq.answer: Class A motorhomes: 6-10 MPG. Class C: 10-14 MPG. Travel trailers reduce tow vehicle MPG by 30-50%. Diesel pushers tend to get slightly better mileage than gas models.

faq.question: Does driving speed affect RV fuel economy?
faq.answer: Yes, significantly. Most RVs achieve best fuel economy between 55-60 MPH. Every 5 MPH over 60 is roughly equivalent to paying an extra $0.20-0.30 per gallon.

faq.question: Should I use diesel or gas fuel prices?
faq.answer: Use diesel prices for diesel pushers (Class A diesel), and gas prices for gas motorhomes and tow vehicles. Diesel is typically $0.30-0.80 more per gallon but diesel engines are more fuel-efficient.

tagline: Estimate your RV's value over time
keywords: rv depreciation calculator, rv value estimator, motorhome depreciation, rv resale value
calcFn: var rates = {classA: 0.15, classC: 0.14, travel: 0.12, fifth: 0.13}; var rate = rates[rvType] || 0.14; var firstYearRate = rate + 0.05; var value = purchasePrice; if (years >= 1) value *= (1 - firstYearRate); for (var i = 1; i < years; i++) value *= (1 - rate); var loss = purchasePrice - value; document.getElementById('currentValue').textContent = dollar(value); document.getElementById('totalLoss').textContent = dollar(loss); document.getElementById('yearlyLoss').textContent = dollar(loss / years) + '/yr'; document.getElementById('retainedPct').textContent = pct(value / purchasePrice * 100, 1); document.getElementById('resultTip').textContent = 'RVs typically lose 20% in the first year and 12-15% per year after.';
formulas: Year 1: Value = Purchase Price × (1 - first year rate)
Years 2+: Value = Previous Value × (1 - annual rate)
Class A: ~15%/yr, Class C: ~14%/yr, Travel Trailer: ~12%/yr, Fifth Wheel: ~13%/yr

faq.question: How fast do RVs depreciate?
faq.answer: RVs typically lose 15-20% of their value in the first year and 12-15% annually after that. After 5 years, most RVs retain about 40-55% of their original value.

faq.question: Which type of RV holds value best?
faq.answer: Travel trailers and fifth wheels tend to depreciate slower than motorhomes because they have no engine or drivetrain to maintain. Airstream trailers hold value exceptionally well.

faq.question: When is the best time to buy a used RV?
faq.answer: Fall and winter are the best times to buy — sellers are motivated before storage season. An RV that's 3-5 years old offers the best value as the steepest depreciation has already occurred.

tagline: Estimate campsite costs for your RV trip
keywords: rv campground cost, campsite cost calculator, rv park cost estimator, camping budget
calcFn: var extras = {full: 10, water_electric: 5, dry: 0}; var extra = extras[hookups] || 0; var effective = nightlyRate + extra; var total = effective * nights; document.getElementById('totalCamp').textContent = dollar(total); document.getElementById('avgNight').textContent = dollar(effective) + '/night'; document.getElementById('weeklyRate').textContent = dollar(effective * 7) + '/week'; document.getElementById('resultTip').textContent = 'Tip: Weekly and monthly rates can save 15-30% at most RV parks.';
formulas: Total = (Base Nightly Rate + Hookup Surcharge) × Number of Nights
Full hookups typically add $5-15/night over dry camping

faq.question: How much does an RV campsite cost per night?
faq.answer: Average RV campsite costs range from $25-50 for basic sites to $50-100+ for premium RV resorts with full hookups. National parks average $25-35. Boondocking (dry camping) on public land is often free.

faq.question: What are full hookups?
faq.answer: Full hookups include water, electric (30 or 50 amp), and sewer connections at your campsite. This lets you use all RV systems without relying on onboard tanks or a generator.

faq.question: How can I save on RV camping costs?
faq.answer: Book weekly or monthly rates for 15-30% savings. Use Harvest Hosts, Boondockers Welcome, or free BLM land. Get a Thousand Trails membership for unlimited nights. Camp mid-week for lower rates.

tagline: Calculate cargo capacity and weight distribution
keywords: rv weight calculator, rv cargo capacity, rv payload calculator, gvwr calculator
calcFn: var totalCCC = gvwr - uvw; var pw = passengers * 154; var ww = water * 8.34; var remaining = totalCCC - pw - ww; document.getElementById('ccc').textContent = fmt(totalCCC, 0) + ' lbs'; document.getElementById('passengerWeight').textContent = fmt(pw, 0) + ' lbs'; document.getElementById('waterWeight').textContent = fmt(ww, 0) + ' lbs'; document.getElementById('remainingCargo').textContent = fmt(Math.max(0, remaining), 0) + ' lbs'; var pctUsed = ((pw + ww) / totalCCC * 100); document.getElementById('resultTip').textContent = remaining < 0 ? 'WARNING: Over GVWR by ' + fmt(Math.abs(remaining), 0) + ' lbs!' : fmt(pctUsed, 0) + '% of capacity used by passengers and water.';
formulas: CCC = GVWR – Unloaded Vehicle Weight
Passenger weight: 154 lbs per person (RVIA standard)
Water weight: 8.34 lbs per gallon
Remaining cargo = CCC – passenger weight – water weight

faq.question: What is GVWR?
faq.answer: Gross Vehicle Weight Rating (GVWR) is the maximum total weight your RV can safely carry including the vehicle itself, passengers, cargo, water, propane, and all fluids. Exceeding GVWR is dangerous and illegal.

faq.question: How much does water weigh in an RV?
faq.answer: Water weighs 8.34 pounds per gallon. A full 75-gallon fresh water tank adds 625 lbs. Many RVers travel with tanks only half-full to save weight and fuel.

faq.question: What is the average RV cargo carrying capacity?
faq.answer: CCC varies widely: Travel trailers typically have 1,000-3,000 lbs CCC, Class C motorhomes 1,500-3,500 lbs, and Class A motorhomes 2,000-5,000 lbs. Always check your specific RV's sticker.

tagline: Size your RV solar system for off-grid camping
keywords: rv solar calculator, rv solar panel sizing, boondocking solar, rv solar system
calcFn: var volts = parseFloat(batteryVolts) || 12; var dailyWh = dailyUse * volts; var wattsNeeded = dailyWh / sunHours / 0.85; var panels100 = Math.ceil(wattsNeeded / 100); var battAh = Math.ceil(dailyUse * 2); document.getElementById('wattsNeeded').textContent = fmt(wattsNeeded, 0) + 'W'; document.getElementById('panels').textContent = panels100 + ' panels'; document.getElementById('batteryAh').textContent = fmt(battAh, 0) + ' Ah'; document.getElementById('dailyWh').textContent = fmt(dailyWh, 0) + ' Wh'; document.getElementById('resultTip').textContent = 'Battery bank sized for 2 days autonomy (50% depth of discharge).';
formulas: Daily Wh = Daily Ah × System Voltage
Solar Watts = Daily Wh ÷ Peak Sun Hours ÷ 0.85 (efficiency)
Battery Bank = Daily Ah × 2 (for 50% DoD and 1 day reserve)

faq.question: How many solar panels does an RV need?
faq.answer: Most RVers need 200-600W of solar. Light users (LED lights, phone charging): 200W. Moderate (TV, fridge): 400W. Heavy (AC, microwave): 800W+. Start by calculating your daily amp-hour usage.

faq.question: What are peak sun hours?
faq.answer: Peak sun hours represent the equivalent hours of full-intensity sunlight per day. In the US Southwest: 6-7 hours. Pacific Northwest: 3-4 hours. National average: 4-5 hours.

faq.question: Can I run AC on RV solar?
faq.answer: A small AC unit draws 1,200-1,500W. To run AC, you'd need at least 1,500W of solar with a large lithium battery bank (400+ Ah). It's possible but expensive. Most RVers use solar for everything except AC.

tagline: Estimate annual insurance premiums for your RV
keywords: rv insurance cost, rv insurance calculator, motorhome insurance estimate, rv coverage cost
calcFn: var baseRates = {classA: 0.015, classC: 0.012, travel: 0.008, fifth: 0.009}; var baseRate = baseRates[rvType] || 0.012; if (fullTime === 'full') baseRate *= 1.4; var annual = Math.max(rvValue * baseRate, 500); document.getElementById('annual').textContent = dollar(annual); document.getElementById('monthly').textContent = dollar(annual / 12); document.getElementById('perTrip').textContent = dollar(annual / 4); document.getElementById('resultTip').textContent = fullTime === 'full' ? 'Full-time rates are typically 30-50% higher than part-time.' : 'Bundle with auto insurance for 5-15% savings.';
formulas: Annual Premium ≈ RV Value × Base Rate
Class A: ~1.5%, Class C: ~1.2%, Travel Trailer: ~0.8%, Fifth Wheel: ~0.9%
Full-time multiplier: 1.3-1.5× part-time rates

faq.question: How much does RV insurance cost?
faq.answer: RV insurance typically costs $1,000-3,000/year for motorhomes and $500-1,500/year for towable RVs. Full-time RV insurance costs 30-50% more due to increased exposure.

faq.question: Do I need special insurance for full-time RV living?
faq.answer: Yes. Standard RV policies assume part-time use. Full-time policies include personal liability, personal property coverage, and emergency expense coverage similar to homeowner's insurance.

faq.question: What does RV insurance cover?
faq.answer: RV insurance typically covers collision, comprehensive (theft, weather), liability, uninsured motorist, personal belongings, and emergency expenses. Full-timer policies add loss of use and total loss replacement.

tagline: Calculate total road trip costs including fuel and camping
keywords: rv trip cost calculator, rv road trip budget, rv travel cost estimator, rv vacation cost
calcFn: var fuel = (miles / mpg) * gasPrice; var camp = nights * campRate; var food = (nights + 1) * foodBudget; var total = fuel + camp + food; var days = nights + 1; document.getElementById('fuelTotal').textContent = dollar(fuel); document.getElementById('campTotal').textContent = dollar(camp); document.getElementById('foodTotal').textContent = dollar(food); document.getElementById('grandTotal').textContent = dollar(total); document.getElementById('perDay').textContent = dollar(total / days); document.getElementById('perMile').textContent = dollar(total / miles);
formulas: Fuel Cost = (Miles ÷ MPG) × Gas Price
Campground Cost = Nights × Nightly Rate
Food Cost = Days × Daily Food Budget
Total = Fuel + Campground + Food

faq.question: How much does an RV trip cost per day?
faq.answer: A typical RV trip costs $150-300/day including fuel, campgrounds, and food. Budget travelers can manage $100/day with boondocking and cooking meals. Luxury RV resorts can push costs to $400+/day.

faq.question: Is RV travel cheaper than hotels?
faq.answer: For families of 3+, RV travel is often cheaper when you factor in avoiding restaurants and hotel rooms. Solo or couples may find it comparable to budget hotels. The break-even point is usually around 30-40 travel days per year.

faq.question: What hidden costs should I budget for?
faq.answer: Common extras include: propane ($20-40 to fill), dump station fees ($10-25), laundry ($5-10/load), tolls, generator fuel, and entertainment. Add 15-20% to your estimated budget for these.

tagline: Find the right generator wattage for your RV
keywords: rv generator size calculator, rv generator wattage, camping generator calculator, rv power needs
calcFn: var acW = parseFloat(ac) * 1800; var mwW = parseFloat(microwave) * 1000; var fridgeW = parseFloat(fridge) * 600; var running = acW + mwW + fridgeW + otherWatts; var starting = running + (acW > 0 ? 1200 : 0); var genRec = starting <= 2000 ? '2,000W Inverter' : starting <= 3500 ? '3,500W Inverter' : starting <= 5500 ? '5,500W Conventional' : starting <= 7500 ? '7,500W Conventional' : '10,000W+ Conventional'; var fuelRate = running / 1000 * 0.15; document.getElementById('runningWatts').textContent = fmt(running, 0) + 'W'; document.getElementById('startingWatts').textContent = fmt(starting, 0) + 'W'; document.getElementById('genSize').textContent = genRec; document.getElementById('fuelPerHr').textContent = fmt(fuelRate, 1) + ' gal/hr';
formulas: Running watts = sum of all appliance wattages
Starting watts = running watts + AC startup surge (~1200W)
Generator size should exceed starting watts by 10-20%
Fuel use ≈ 0.15 gal/hr per 1000W running load

faq.question: What size generator do I need for my RV?
faq.answer: Most RVs with one AC unit need a 3,000-3,500W generator. Two AC units require 5,500W+. If you only need lights, fridge, and charging, a 2,000W inverter generator is sufficient.

faq.question: Inverter vs conventional generator for RV?
faq.answer: Inverter generators are quieter (50-60 dB vs 70-80 dB), produce cleaner power for electronics, and are more fuel-efficient. They cost more but are preferred at campgrounds with noise restrictions.

faq.question: How much fuel does an RV generator use?
faq.answer: A 3,500W generator at 50% load uses about 0.25-0.5 gallons per hour. Running a generator 8 hours a day costs roughly $10-15 in fuel at current prices.
